ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chestnut entry; no specific dried Chinese chestnut entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chestnuts broadly; no dried-specific sub-code identified. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried Chinese chestnut; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried Chinese chestnuts is approximately 30g (about 4–6 pieces), providing roughly 109 kcal and 23.9g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Dried Chinese Chestnut — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030026 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this source. Sugar and dietary fibre values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170186, Chestnuts, Chinese, dried) where HPB does not report them.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for dried Chinese chestnut was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 60 is estimated from the published GI of boiled/roasted chestnuts (GI ~60, medium category) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chestnuts. Drying concentrates carbohydrates but the starch gelatinisation state is similar; GI is conservatively retained at 60. GL per serving = (60 × 23.9g available carbohydrate per 30g serving) / 100 = 14.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5mg/100g) and high potassium (726mg/100g) yield a strongly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH diet targets.
hypertension.guidance: Dried Chinese chestnuts are suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions (30g); their high potassium content supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation, but the high total carbohydrate load (79.8g/100g) means large servings should be avoided to prevent weight gain, which can elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high carbohydrate density (79.8g/100g) with a medium GI (~60); dietary fibre (~8.1g/100g) partially moderates glycaemic response but does not fully offset the glucose load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit dried Chinese chestnut intake to 30g per serving, pair with protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and account for the concentrated carbohydrate density relative to fresh chestnuts when carbohydrate counting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.34g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium (726mg/100g) support a favourable cardiovascular profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried Chinese chestnuts are low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making them a heart-friendly nut option in moderate portions (30g); their high potassium content supports arterial health, though the high carbohydrate density warrants portion control to avoid excess caloric intake in individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, no trans fat, and meaningful dietary fibre (~8.1g/100g) support gut microbiome health and immune modulation relevant to RA management.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried Chinese chestnuts are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a low-saturated-fat, plant-based carbohydrate source; however, they are not a significant omega-3 source and should be paired with fatty fish or walnuts for stronger anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried Chinese chestnuts (干栗子, Castanea mollissima) are whole chestnuts that have been dehydrated to remove moisture, resulting in a dark brown, firm, and chewy product with a concentrated sweet, nutty flavour. The drying process significantly increases carbohydrate density to approximately 79.8g per 100g compared to roughly 45g in fresh chestnuts, making portion awareness essential. They are a staple ingredient in Chinese cuisine, commonly used in braised pork belly dishes, rice stuffing, soups, and festive preparations. Nutritionally, dried Chinese chestnuts are notable for their very high potassium content (726mg/100g), low sodium (5mg/100g), negligible saturated fat (0.34g/100g), and meaningful dietary fibre (~8.1g/100g). They are cholesterol-free and suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free diets. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium category), yielding a glycaemic load of ~14.3 per 30g serving. Clinically, they are well-suited for individuals with hypertension due to their favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, but require careful portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given the high carbohydrate density. Their low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile make them a heart-friendly snack when consumed in moderation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dried Chinese chestnuts?
Are dried chestnuts high in carbs?
What is the GI of chestnuts?
Are chestnuts good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030026, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Sugar, dietary fibre, and fatty acid breakdown supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170186, Chestnuts, Chinese, dried), an approved secondary source. GI estimated from closest analogous chestnut entry in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), documented as analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre (~8.1g/100g), sugar (10.6g/100g), saturated fat (0.34g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.63g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.72g/100g), and selected B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6), phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water content are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170186 (Chestnuts, Chinese, dried) as HPB does not report these fields. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these values are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FDC 170186 for this specific food item as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; chestnuts are a plant food and inherently contain no cholesterol (naturally zero). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero in dried chestnuts per USDA FDC 170186; fresh chestnuts contain vitamin C but drying destroys it substantially. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero in plant-based dried nuts per USDA FDC 170186. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC 170186; no industrial hydrogenation involved. 8. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chestnut entry and Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for dried Chinese chestnut was found in approved sources. 9. Ontology codes use closest defensible matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for dried Chinese chestnut exists in published ontology releases as of 2026-04-16.
